summary: I was born in Xieng Khouang Province, Laos, in 1954. This is where my early life began before the turmoil of the Secret War affected my family deeply. My father died during that conflict, which changed everything for us. Xieng Khouang is my homeland and the place I carry in my heart.

I grew up in Laos, specifically in Xieng Khouang Province, during a very difficult time. The Secret War impacted my family directly, as my father fought and died there. In 1984, my family and I left Laos, crossing the Mekong River to escape the conflict. Laos is where my roots are, but I had to leave for safety and a better life.

The Secret War in Laos was a tragic period for me and my family. My father was a soldier in that war fighting against the communist Vietnamese, and he was killed at a base camp. This loss left me orphaned and deeply saddened. The war forced many of us to flee Laos and seek refuge elsewhere.

I immigrated to the United States in 1987 with my wife and children after spending time as a refugee in Thailand. I first arrived in Seattle, Washington, before moving to Wisconsin and later Minnesota. The U.S. became my new home where I rebuilt my life and now serve as the director of the Volunteers of America Park Elder Center in Minneapolis.

After arriving in the United States, I initially settled in Wisconsin. It was my first home in America before I moved to Minnesota. Wisconsin was important as it was where I began adapting to life in a new country with my family.

Minnesota became my home after Wisconsin, and today I live and work in Minneapolis, Minnesota. I am the director of the Volunteers of America Park Elder Center here. Minnesota has given me opportunities to serve my community and preserve my culture.

I am currently the director of the Volunteers of America Park Elder Center in Minneapolis, Minnesota. This organization allows me to support elders in my community and stay connected to my cultural roots. It is rewarding to give back and help others in this role.

Minneapolis is where I now live and work, directing the Volunteers of America Park Elder Center. It is a city that has welcomed me and my family after our long journey from Laos and Thailand. Here, I continue to share and preserve Hmong culture through my work and music.

In 1984, my family and I crossed the Mekong River to escape Laos during the Secret War. This river marked a dangerous but necessary passage from our homeland to safety in Thailand. Crossing the Mekong was a pivotal moment in our journey as refugees.

After fleeing Laos and crossing the Mekong River in 1984, my family and I lived in Thailand as refugees until 1987. Thailand was a temporary home where we waited before immigrating to the United States. It was during this time that I replaced my broken flute and prepared for a new life.

Seattle was my first stop when I arrived in the United States in 1987. From there, I took a connecting flight to Wisconsin where I settled with my family. Seattle marks the beginning of my new life in America.

During the Secret War, my father fought against the communist Vietnamese forces. This conflict deeply affected my family and led to my father's death. The Vietnamese were the opposing side in the war that shaped my early life and eventual migration.

I am a member of the Hmong people, and our culture is very important to me. The musical instruments like the flute and the qeej are vital parts of our heritage. I hope the younger generation learns to play these instruments to keep our culture alive, and I am willing to teach anyone interested.

The qeej is a traditional Hmong musical instrument that, along with the flute, represents our culture. I often talk about the importance of the qeej and encourage young people to learn how to play it. It is a symbol of our heritage and identity that I want to see preserved.
